questions.question: Define the F-distribution and state its two parameters with notation.
keyPoints: F-distribution is a continuous probability distribution arising from ratio of two independent chi-square variables
Notation: F(df1, df2) or F(m, n) where df1 = numerator degrees of freedom and df2 = denominator degrees of freedom
Both parameters are positive integers determining the shape of the distribution

questions.question: State the mean and variance of F-distribution and conditions for their existence.
keyPoints: Mean = df2/(df2-2), exists when df2 > 2
Variance = [2(df2)²(df1+df2-2)] / [df1(df2-2)²(df2-4)], exists when df2 > 4
When df2 ≤ 2, mean does not exist; when df2 ≤ 4, variance does not exist

questions.question: Explain the relationship between F-distribution and Chi-square distribution.
keyPoints: If X ~ χ²(m) and Y ~ χ²(n) are independent, then (X/m)/(Y/n) ~ F(m,n)
F-distribution is derived from ratio of two chi-square variables
This relationship forms the basis for deriving F-distribution properties

questions.question: Describe the shape of F-distribution and how it changes with increasing degrees of freedom.
keyPoints: F-distribution is always positive (x > 0) and right-skewed
Starts from 0 and extends to positive infinity
As df1 and df2 increase, distribution becomes more symmetric
As df → ∞, F-distribution approaches normal distribution centered at 1

questions.question: Write the formula for F-statistic in testing equality of two variances.
keyPoints: F = s1²/s2² where s1² is larger sample variance and s2² is smaller sample variance
Under H0: σ1² = σ2², this follows F(df1, df2) where df1 = n1-1, df2 = n2-1
Always put larger variance in numerator to get F > 1 for table lookup

questions.question: How is F-distribution used in One-Way ANOVA? Explain the hypothesis being tested.
keyPoints: In ANOVA: F = MSB/MSW where MSB = SSB/df_between and MSW = SSW/df_within
H0: μ1 = μ2 = ... = μk (all group means are equal)
H1: At least one mean differs significantly
If F > F-critical, reject H0 indicating significant differences between group means

questions.question: State the relationship between t-distribution and F-distribution.
keyPoints: If T ~ t(n), then T² ~ F(1, n)
This is because t² is a special case of F-distribution with numerator df = 1
This relationship is useful in connection between t-tests and F-tests in ANOVA

questions.question: Derive the probability density function of F-distribution and explain its properties.
keyPoints: Property 1: Always positive and defined for x > 0 only
Property 2: Right-skewed, becoming symmetric as df increase
Property 3: Mean = df2/(df2-2) for df2 > 2
Property 4: Mode = [(df1-2)/df1] × [df2/(df2+2)] for df1 > 2
Property 5: F → 1 in probability as df1, df2 → ∞

questions.question: Explain the F-test for equality of two population variances. Include hypotheses, test statistic, decision rule, and assumptions.
keyPoints: H0: σ1² = σ2² (variances equal), H1: σ1² ≠ σ2² (two-tailed) or σ1² > σ2² (one-tailed)
Test statistic: F = s1²/s2² with larger variance in numerator
Degrees of freedom: df1 = n1-1, df2 = n2-1
Decision: Reject H0 if F > Fα(df1, df2) for α significance level
Assumptions: Populations must be normally distributed, samples are independent

questions.question: Explain the role of F-distribution in One-Way ANOVA. Derive the F-statistic and explain how it helps in testing equality of means.
keyPoints: ANOVA partitions total variation into variation between groups (SSB) and within groups (SSW)
MSB = SSB/(k-1) where k = number of groups; MSW = SSW/(n-k) where n = total observations
F = MSB/MSW follows F(k-1, n-k) under null hypothesis
If F > F-critical, reject H0: μ1 = μ2 = ... = μk, concluding at least one mean differs
Logic: Large F indicates between-group variation exceeds within-group variation, suggesting true differences

questions.question: Two samples of sizes 8 and 10 are drawn from normal populations with variances s1² = 18.5 and s2² = 8.2. Test at 5% significance level whether the first population has larger variance.
keyPoints: H0: σ1² = σ2², H1: σ1² > σ2² (right-tailed test)
Degrees of freedom: df1 = 7, df2 = 9
Critical value F0.05(7,9) from table ≈ 3.29
Since F(calculated) = 2.26 < 3.29, do not reject H0
Conclusion: No significant evidence that first population variance is larger
